items.title: Trump: Thai and Cambodian leaders will meet | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>The conflict between Thailand and Cambodia has spread along their disputed border, even as both sides say they are open to ceasefire talks. Donald Trump says he's called the leaders of both nations - and that they have agreed to quickly meet to arrange a ceasefire. So far more than 30 people have been killed, and around 160 thousand forced to flee their homes. 

00:00 Trump: Thai and Cambodian leaders will meet
02:36 Andreyka Natalegawa, CSIS
06:47 Georg Matthes, DW Southeast Asia Bureau Chief

For more news go to: http://www.dw.com/en/

items.title: Trump in Scotland while protesters take to the streets and decry his visit | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>US President Donald Trump is in Scotland for a  five-day trip that will also include meetings with EU chief Ursula von der Leyen and UK Prime Minister Keir Starmer. Thousands of police have been deployed to manage the protests planned for Trump's trip , including enviromental demonstrations against his new golf course he is due to visit in Aberdeenshire. Upon his arrival in Scotland, Trump spoke to reporters and slammed Europe's wind energy and immigration policies.

For more news go to: http://www.dw.com/en/

items.title: CSD: Thousands march in Berlin Pride parade | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>Tens of thousands of people are marching through Berlin for the annual LGBTQ+ Pride parade. Here the event is known as Christopher Street Day, or CSD. This year, it's not just a celebration, but a call to action. Marchers are voicing concern over growing homophobia, rallying under the slogan: "Never Silent Again." Some are protesting against attacks on Pride events in smaller German cities by far-right extremists. 


For more news go to: http://www.dw.com/en/

items.title: Typhoon Co-may sparks deadly floods in Philippines | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>Dozens of people have been killed in the Philippines in floods caused by a typhoon that hit the country on Friday.
Officials say at least 25 people died in the extreme weather, mainly from flash floods and toppled trees. A further eight people have been reported missing. Over 80 towns and cities have declared emergencies. Typhoon Co-may made landfall early on Friday morning, before weakening into a tropical storm.

00:00 Floods in Philippines leave dozens dead
00:31 Journalist Dave Grunebaum reports from an evacuation center at an elementary school in Pasig City

For more news go to: http://www.dw.com/en/

items.title: Mass hunger in Gaza: What's the political price for Israel? | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>The United Nations says nearly a third of people in Gaza are going days without food. The UN's World Food Programme is warning that some 90 thousand women and children are in urgent need of treatment for malnourishment. Nearly half a million people are expected to face what it calls 'catastrophic hunger' by September. Israel controls what is allowed into Gaza. It denies there are restrictions on getting aid into the territory and blames Hamas for the crisis.

00:00 UN says one in three Gazans goes days without food
02:45 DW speaks with Fawaz Gerges, Professor of Middle Eastern Politics and International Relations at the London School of Economics

For more news go to: http://www.dw.com/en/

items.title: Carbon trading in Africa sparks controversy: Who benefits? | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>Africa is fast becoming a major player in the global carbon market as countries in the global north ramp up efforts to compensate their carbon emmissions.  
The markets are supposed to incentivize industries to cut back emissions. But are they simply letting big polluters off the hook - shifting the burden to poor communities?
Here's how it works: Companies buy carbon credits, allowing them to offset their emmissions by paying for projects that reduce carbon elsewhere - like planting trees, and protecting forests.

00:00 Carbon trading in Africa
00:35 Carbon deal in DRC leaves locals behind
06:31 Carbon trading supports Sierra Leone's Tiwai island
07:51 DW speaks with Sam Mugume Koojo, Assistant Commissioner of Uganda's Ministry of Finance
14:15 Carbon trading plan divides Kenyan communities
19:01 DW speaks with Amos Burudi Wemanya of Greenpeace Africa Nairobi

For more news go to: http://www.dw.com/en/

items.title: Thailand-Cambodia conflict: Could US and China become involved? | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>Tens of thousands have been displaced since cross-border fighting broke out on Thursday and frantic efforts have been underway to deescalate. Tonight we talk to our correspondents on the ground and ask how the US and China could get involved. 

"Thailand and Cambodia could be moving towards war", warns the acting Thai Prime Minister. Tensions over a decades-old border dispute have been flaring up between the neighbors, who have been exchanging artillery fire for a second consecutive day. On Friday evening, a Thai border commander declared martial law in eight districts along the frontier citing 'Cambodia's use of force to enter Thai territory'. Malaysia, Thailand's neighbor to the south has weighed in to de-escalate, putting a ceasefire proposal on the table that's now under consideration. 

Chapters:
0:00 The threat of war
4:19 Tommy Walker, Journalist
9:49 Joshua Kurlantzick, Council on Foreign Relations
13:12 Georg Matthes, DW Southeast Asia Bureau Chief

For more news go to: http://www.dw.com/en/

items.title: Trump lands in Scotland for private trip combining business, politics and golf | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>US President Donald Trump has arrived in Scotland for a private trip that's set to blend business, politics, and golf.

Trump is due to hold talks with UK Prime Minister Keir Starmer and Scottish first minister John Swinney. He is also celebrating the opening of his new golf course in Aberdeenshire - which has sparked outcry over environmental concerns. Around 5,000 police are expected to be deployed to handle protests against Trump's visit, costing British taxpayers millions.

Chapters:
0:00 Trump arrives in Scotland
1:47 Ethan Bearman, Attorney & political commentator

items.title: Gaza mother of six struggles to feed her children | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>This Palestinian mother in Gaza is struggling to feed her six starving children, two of whom are already malnourished — the others so thin that their "bones are protruding."

Gaza officials say that 9 more people have died of malnutrition in the past 24 hours, bringing the total since the war began to 122.

items.title: Thailand says it agrees in principal to ceasefire proposal as tens of thousands flee | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>Thailand has said it agrees in principal with a Malaysian proposal for a ceasefire between Thai and Cambodian forces -- and will consider the offer. Tens of thousands of people have fled their homes on the Thai-Cambodian border after intense fighting between the neighbouring countries. Sixteen people have so far been killed in the clashes that erupted on Thursday over a long-running border dispute.

Chapters:
0:00 Clashes on the Thai-Cambodian border

items.title: How ICE raids send shockwaves through local communities | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>In the United States, the Trump administration is ramping up its deportation campaign of undocumented migrants. Immigration and Customs Enforcement agents have been conducting mass raids nationwide. DW's Washington bureau chief, Ines Pohl, reports now from the farming region of Oxnard in California, which is known as the "breadbasket of America". Farmers there say most of their workers are no longer showing up, out of fear of being detained.

For more news go to: http://www.dw.com/en/
